section.title: A Prayer from the Cave
section.sectionRationale: Titled a Maskil of David when he was in the cave, this is the most explicitly autobiographical lament in the closing Davidic cluster of Book V, a short, tightly structured prayer that moves from isolation and abandonment to the bold confession, You are my refuge, my portion in the land of the living, a pattern for honest prayer in seasons of total human abandonment.
section.thesis: Alone in a literal cave with no human ally in sight, David pours out his complaint without pretense, and precisely there, where no one else cares for his soul, declares the LORD his refuge and portion in the land of the living, turning isolation itself into the occasion for the boldest confession of trust.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the honest pouring out of complaint amid isolation and hidden snares, verses 1-4; and the turn to bold confession and plea for rescue, ending in praise, verses 5-7.

units.label: I pour out my complaint before Him

verses.text: I cry aloud to the LORD; I lift my voice to the LORD for mercy.
I pour out my {{siach|complaint}} before Him; I reveal my trouble to Him.
Although my spirit grows faint within me, You know my way. Along the path I travel they have hidden a snare for me.
Look to my right and see; no one attends to me. There is no refuge for me; no one cares for my soul.

units.label: You are my refuge, my portion
units.note: bold confession and plea for rescue

verses.text: I cry to You, O LORD: "You are my {{machaseh|refuge}}, my portion in the land of the living."
Listen to my cry, for I am brought quite low. Rescue me from my pursuers, for they are too strong for me.
Free my soul from prison, that I may praise Your name. The righteous will gather around me because of Your goodness to me.

siach.gloss: Complaint, meditation, or concern, spoken freely and without pretense. David does not hide his trouble from God but pours it out completely, modeling honest, unfiltered prayer even in a season of total isolation.

machaseh.gloss: Refuge, shelter, a place of safety, the same word used in Psalm 46:1. Here, surrounded by a literal rocky hideout yet abandoned by every human ally, David declares that the LORD Himself, not the cave, is his true refuge and portion.

basic.context: Composed, according to its title, while David hid in a cave fleeing King Saul, likely at Adullam or En-gedi, this short psalm captures the moment when every human support has failed and David is left with nothing but God to cry out to, and finds that God is enough.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes David's specific, historical experience of isolation and danger in the cave; it prescribes the abiding pattern of pouring out complaint honestly before God even in total abandonment, and of finding in Him alone sufficient refuge and portion.
basic.originalLanguages: David pours out his siach, complaint, holding nothing back from God even in the depths of isolation. He declares the LORD his machaseh, refuge, the same word used elsewhere of a sheltering cliff, fitting for a man hiding in an actual rocky cave yet trusting God, not the rock, as his true shelter.
basic.typology: David's cry from confinement, bring my soul out of prison, verse 7, anticipates both the deliverance language of Isaiah 61:1 and its fulfillment when Jesus reads that very passage and announces liberty to the captives, Luke 4:18.
basic.christ: Christ, like David, was abandoned by His closest companions in His darkest hour, no one stood with me, but the Lord stood by me, 2 Timothy 4:16-17, and His resurrection surrounds Him at last with the righteous, Hebrews 2:12, just as David anticipates the righteous gathering around him because of God's goodness.
basic.god: God is shown as refuge and portion precisely when every human refuge has failed, One who knows the way even when no one else notices or cares, and who can free a soul from the tightest prison and turn private anguish into shared praise.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 46:1 uses the same word machaseh, refuge, of God as ever-present help. 2 Timothy 4:16-17 echoes David's abandonment yet confidence that the Lord stood by him. Isaiah 61:1 and Luke 4:18 fulfill the plea to be freed from prison and brought into liberty.
basic.oneStory: David's cry from the cave, abandoned by every human ally yet confident that God alone is refuge and portion, anticipates Christ's own greater abandonment and deliverance, and promises every believer that isolation is never the last word when the LORD is the portion still held onto.

context.literary: A tightly structured lament in two movements, honest complaint amid isolation, verses 1-4, and bold confession turning to plea and anticipated praise, verses 5-7, the shortest and most autobiographically specific psalm in this closing Davidic cluster.
context.historical: The title, A Maskil of David, when he was in the cave, points to either the cave of Adullam, 1 Samuel 22:1-2, or En-gedi, 1 Samuel 24:1-3, both refuges during Saul's pursuit of David, when he had lost home, position, and nearly all human companionship.
context.canonical: The third psalm in the cluster of Davidic laments closing Book V, Psalms 140-143, its intense personal isolation deepens the sequence's honesty before turning, in Psalm 143, toward renewed hope and guidance.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model honest, unfiltered prayer in a season of total human abandonment, and to turn that very isolation into the occasion for bold confession that God alone is sufficient refuge and portion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific historical crisis, hiding from Saul with no human help in sight; it prescribes the abiding pattern of pouring out complaint honestly to God and finding Him sufficient even when every human support has vanished.

debates.question: Does David's complaint, no one cares for my soul, verse 4, contradict the fact that he had loyal followers with him in the cave, 1 Samuel 22:2?

views.label: The statement reflects David's felt experience of isolation in that moment, a subjective cry of abandonment rather than a literal claim that not a single person was present
views.case: Laments regularly use hyperbolic, emotionally honest language to express the depth of felt isolation, and even surrounded by followers a leader can feel that no one truly understands or carries his particular burden.

views.label: The psalm may reflect an earlier, more isolated moment before those followers gathered to him, or a specific danger in which those present could offer no real help
views.case: 1 Samuel 22:1-2 describes distressed, discontented, and indebted men gathering to David, hardly a company able to rescue him from Saul's forces, so David's felt sense that no one could truly help or defend him may be substantially accurate even with people nearby.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm gives permission for radically honest prayer about feelings of abandonment, even when those feelings do not capture every detail of one's actual circumstances, because God receives such honesty rather than requiring polished accuracy.

study.originalLanguages: David pours out his siach, complaint, holding nothing back from God in the depths of isolation. He calls the LORD his machaseh, refuge, the same word elsewhere describing a sheltering cliff, fitting for a man hiding in an actual cave yet trusting God, not the rock itself, as his true shelter.

typology.type: Bring my soul out of prison, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Isaiah 61:1's promise of liberty to captives, fulfilled when Jesus reads this very passage and announces release, Luke 4:18.

study.christ: Christ, like David, was abandoned by His closest companions in His darkest hour, no one stood with me, but the Lord stood by me, 2 Timothy 4:16-17, and His resurrection surrounds Him with the righteous at last, Hebrews 2:12, just as David anticipates the righteous gathering around him because of God's goodness.
study.god: God is refuge and portion precisely when every human refuge has failed, the One who knows the way even when no one else notices or cares, able to free a soul from the tightest prison and turn private anguish into shared praise.

crossReferences.note: Uses the same Hebrew word machaseh, refuge, of God as an ever-present help in trouble.
Paul's own experience of abandonment, no one stood with me, echoes David's isolation yet shared confidence that the Lord stood by him.
Promises liberty to captives, fulfilled when Jesus reads this passage and announces release in Luke 4:18.

study.oneStory: David's cry from the cave, abandoned by every human ally yet confident that God alone is refuge and portion, anticipates Christ's own greater abandonment and deliverance, and promises every believer that isolation is never the last word when the LORD remains the portion held onto.

quiz.q: What does the title of this psalm tell us about its setting?

options.text: It was written by David when he was in the cave, likely while fleeing King Saul.
options.note: The title, A Maskil of David, when he was in the cave, points directly to David's flight from Saul, 1 Samuel 22 or 24.

options.text: It was written by David during a time of peace and prosperity in Jerusalem.
options.note: The title specifically locates the psalm during a cave hiding, a season of danger, not peace or prosperity.

options.text: It was written after David became king over all Israel.
options.note: The cave setting reflects David's fugitive years before his kingship, not his later reign.

options.text: It was written by an anonymous exile in Babylon.
options.note: The title attributes the psalm to David specifically in a cave, not to a later, anonymous exile setting.

quiz.q: What does David do with his trouble in verse 2?

options.text: He pours out his complaint before God and reveals his trouble to Him fully.
options.note: David models complete, honest disclosure of his distress directly to God.

options.text: He hides his trouble from God out of shame.
options.note: The verse describes the opposite, complete openness with God about his trouble.

options.text: He only complains to his human companions, not to God.
options.note: The complaint is explicitly directed to God, before Him, not merely to people.

options.text: He pretends everything is fine and suppresses his real feelings.
options.note: The psalm is marked by unusually honest, unfiltered expression, not suppression of feeling.

quiz.q: What does David observe about his human support in verse 4?

options.text: That no one attends to him, no refuge remains, and no one cares for his soul.
options.note: This stark statement of isolation captures the emotional depth of David's felt abandonment.

options.text: That he is surrounded by an army of loyal soldiers.
options.note: The verse expresses the felt absence of human support, not the presence of an army.

options.text: That King Saul has become his ally.
options.note: Saul is David's pursuer in this narrative, not an ally; the verse expresses isolation, not reconciliation.

options.text: That his family has come to rescue him.
options.note: The verse specifically states that no one cares for his soul, the opposite of rescue by family.

quiz.q: What bold confession does David make in verse 5?

options.text: "You are my refuge, my portion in the land of the living."
options.note: This declaration turns David's isolation into an occasion for confident trust in God alone.

options.text: "I have no hope left in this cave."
options.note: The verse is a confession of confident trust, not despair without hope.

options.text: "My own strength will save me from Saul."
options.note: David explicitly turns to God as refuge, not to his own strength.

options.text: "I will abandon my trust in the LORD."
options.note: The verse affirms trust in the LORD as refuge, the opposite of abandoning that trust.

quiz.q: What does David anticipate happening once God rescues him, according to verse 7?

options.text: That his soul will be freed from prison so he may praise God, and the righteous will gather around him because of God's goodness.
options.note: David expects his personal deliverance to become an occasion for shared praise among the righteous.

options.text: That he will keep his deliverance completely private and never mention it to anyone.
options.note: The verse anticipates public praise and gathering of the righteous, not private silence.

options.text: That he will take revenge on Saul once free.
options.note: The verse focuses on praise and communal gathering, not revenge against his pursuer.

options.text: That he will remain hidden in the cave permanently.
options.note: The verse anticipates rescue and freedom from confinement, not permanent hiding.

questions: David pours out his complaint to God without pretense, holding nothing back. Is there a trouble you have been hiding from God rather than pouring out honestly before Him?

David feels that no one attends to him or cares for his soul, even though, historically, others were nearby. Where do you feel isolated right now, and how does it help to know God receives that feeling honestly rather than requiring you to first prove it accurate?

In his lowest, most isolated moment, David declares, You are my refuge, my portion in the land of the living. What competes with God for the place of your refuge and portion when life feels like a cave?
David asks God to free his soul from prison so that he may praise God's name. What confinement, physical, emotional, or spiritual, are you asking God to free you from right now?

David expects that his personal deliverance will draw the righteous around him in shared praise. How might sharing your own story of God's help become an encouragement to others facing similar isolation?

translationNotes.note: A Maskil is probably a musical or liturgical term whose precise meaning is uncertain, used as a heading for several psalms including 32, 42, 44-45, 52-55, 74, 78, 88-89, and this one.
Look to my right and see reflects the ancient custom of a defender or advocate standing at a person's right hand; David observes that no such defender is present for him.

Portion in the land of the living echoes the language used of the Levites' inheritance in Numbers 18:20, where the LORD Himself, not land, is their portion, language David applies personally to his own circumstance.

meta.notes: BSB text retrieved via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com (local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error). No em or en dashes were present in the source text for this psalm. A Desiring God 2014 conference message on Psalm 142 and 57, 'Pure and Simple Devotion,' was found via WebSearch but is delivered by Ben Stuart, a guest speaker not part of the specified trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson), so it was not used and sermon status is recorded as none rather than substituting an untrusted source.
